title: The Power of a Name
carnegie_principle: Remember that a person's name is to that person the sweetest and most important sound in any language.
modern_context: Personalizing digital openers (Slack, Email, LinkedIn) to cut through the noise.
practice_prompt: You are messaging a busy stakeholder, Sarah, for the first time. Open the conversation naturally using her name and a clear reason for reaching out.

title: Smile on Camera
carnegie_principle: Smile.
modern_context: Projecting warmth and presence in high-stakes video calls even when you're tired.
practice_prompt: Join this mock Zoom meeting and introduce yourself. Focus on maintaining a genuine smile and steady eye contact with the camera lens.
success_criteria: Eyes fixed on camera, not the screen

title: Kill the Filler
carnegie_principle: Be a good listener. Encourage others to talk about themselves.
modern_context: Using strategic pauses to eliminate 'um', 'ah', and 'like' for more authority.
practice_prompt: Explain your current project in 60 seconds. Every time you want to say a filler word, take a breath and pause instead.

title: Become Genuinely Interested
carnegie_principle: Become genuinely interested in other people.
modern_context: Active listening and room-reading in a hybrid work environment.
practice_prompt: Ask three insightful follow-up questions to the AI avatar after they describe their weekend trip to Japan.

title: Let Others Talk
carnegie_principle: Let the other person do a great deal of the talking.
modern_context: Facilitating vs dominating meetings. Learning when to yield the floor.
practice_prompt: You are leading a stand-up. Practice a transition that invites a quieter teammate to share their progress without putting them on the spot.

title: Avoid Criticism
carnegie_principle: Don't criticize, condemn or complain.
modern_context: Delivering radical candor via the SBI (Situation, Behavior, Impact) framework without triggering defensiveness.
practice_prompt: A colleague missed a deadline. Give them feedback using the SBI framework without being critical of their character.

title: Talk in Terms of Their Interests
carnegie_principle: Talk in terms of the other person's interests.
modern_context: Stakeholder management. Framing your request as a win for their specific KPIs.
practice_prompt: Pitch a new software tool to a CFO who only cares about cost reduction and ROI. Frame your entire pitch around those two points.

title: The Sincere Compliment
carnegie_principle: Give honest and sincere appreciation.
modern_context: Specific vs generic praise. How to compliment a peer so it actually lands.
practice_prompt: Think of a colleague who's done something well in the past week. Give a 30-second voice note with one specific, sincere compliment. Name the detail. Avoid flattery.

title: Admit Mistakes Quickly
carnegie_principle: If you are wrong, admit it quickly and emphatically.
modern_context: Building psychological safety. Leading with vulnerability when a project slips.
practice_prompt: You made a mistake in the quarterly report. Practice a 30-second Slack video message to the team admitting the error and outlining the fix.

title: The 2-Minute Pitch
carnegie_principle: Dramatize your ideas.
modern_context: Hook → Insight → Ask. The foundational structure of persuasive leadership.
practice_prompt: You have two minutes with a high-level executive. Pitch your vision for the company's next AI initiative.

title: Slack Tone: The Digital Handshake
carnegie_principle: Make the other person feel important - and do it sincerely.
modern_context: Mastering the nuance of async text communication. Avoiding 'Hey' without context.
practice_prompt: Rewrite a blunt request for data into a warm, context-rich message that makes the recipient feel valued.

title: LinkedIn Voice: Professional Sincerity
carnegie_principle: Arouse in the other person an eager want.
modern_context: Writing a cold reach-out that isn't spam. Building genuine bridges.
practice_prompt: Draft a personalized connection request to a mentor you admire. Explain why you want to connect in terms of their work.

title: Async Video: Authority + Warmth
carnegie_principle: Be hearty in your approbation and lavish in your praise.
modern_context: Using Loom or Slack video for feedback. Using your face and voice to build trust async.
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second video update on a successful project milestone. Focus on praising the team's specific contributions.

title: Email That Lands
carnegie_principle: Call attention to people's mistakes indirectly.
modern_context: Writing tough emails that don't burn bridges. Managing up via text.
practice_prompt: Email a manager who forgot to include you in a critical meeting. Point it out without being accusatory.

title: The Power of One-on-Ones
carnegie_principle: Show respect for the other person's opinions. Never say, 'You're wrong.'
modern_context: Structuring high-trust recurring meetings. Listening for what's NOT being said.
practice_prompt: Start a 1:1 with a direct report who has been underperforming lately. Create a safe space for them to speak first.

title: The Art of Disagreement
carnegie_principle: The only way to get the best of an argument is to avoid it.
modern_context: Navigating technical or strategic disagreements in public channels.
practice_prompt: Disagree with a colleague's technical proposal in a mock Slack thread. Validate their points before offering your alternative.

title: Leading Through Uncertainty
carnegie_principle: Give the other person a fine reputation to live up to.
modern_context: Communicating during a pivot or reorg. Keeping the team focused on the mission.
practice_prompt: Give a short 'all-hands' update about a difficult project pivot. Reaffirm the team's strengths.

title: The Sincere No
carnegie_principle: Make the other person happy about doing the thing you suggest.
modern_context: Setting boundaries without losing influence. Saying 'no' to a project but 'yes' to the person.
practice_prompt: Turn down a request to join a new committee while maintaining the relationship with the person who asked.

title: Handling Digital Confrontation
carnegie_principle: Begin in a friendly way.
modern_context: De-escalating a heated thread. When to take it off-line.
practice_prompt: A client is angry in a shared Slack channel. Write a response that de-escalates and moves the conversation to a 1:1 call.

title: Mastering the Hybrid Meeting
carnegie_principle: Ask questions instead of giving direct orders.
modern_context: Ensuring remote participants feel as included as those in the room.
practice_prompt: You are facilitating a hybrid brainstorm. Explicitly pull in a remote participant who hasn't spoken yet.

title: Authentic Executive Presence
carnegie_principle: Let the other person feel that the idea is his or hers.
modern_context: Leading without appearing to command. The 'Inception' method of leadership.
practice_prompt: Guide an AI executive to your proposed strategy using only Socratic questioning.

title: High-Stakes Negotiation
carnegie_principle: Try honestly to see things from the other person's point of view.
modern_context: Salary or contract negotiation. Finding the 'Third Way' where everyone wins.
practice_prompt: Negotiate a 15% budget increase with a skeptical AI department head.

title: The Sincerity of Leadership
carnegie_principle: Use encouragement. Make the fault seem easy to correct.
modern_context: Coaching a high-performer through a slump. Moving from 'Manager' to 'Coach'.
practice_prompt: Deliver a coaching session to a star player who just failed a major launch.

title: Complex Conflict Resolution
carnegie_principle: Be sympathetic with the other person's ideas and desires.
modern_context: Mediating a deep rift between two powerful stakeholders.
practice_prompt: Practice the first 5 minutes of a mediation session between two AI co-founders who aren't speaking.

title: Visionary Storytelling
carnegie_principle: Appeal to the nobler motives.
modern_context: Rallying a global team around a difficult, long-term mission.
practice_prompt: Deliver a 'Founder's Vision' talk for a project that won't show results for 3 years.

title: The Radical Candor Reset
carnegie_principle: Talk about your own mistakes before criticizing the other person.
modern_context: Correcting toxic culture by leading with your own growth areas.
practice_prompt: Address a team-wide burnout issue by first sharing your own struggles with work-life balance.

title: Negotiating with Power
carnegie_principle: Let the other person save face.
modern_context: How to win a strategic argument with your CEO without embarrassing them.
practice_prompt: Propose a complete reversal of a CEO's pet project in a private meeting.

title: Public Authenticity
carnegie_principle: Dramatize your ideas.
modern_context: Handling Q&A in front of 500+ people. Projecting calm under fire.
practice_prompt: Answer three difficult, confrontational audience questions after a keynote.

title: Building Lasting Influence
carnegie_principle: Praise the slightest improvement and praise every improvement.
modern_context: Cultivating long-term champions in your organization.
practice_prompt: Practice a series of small, authentic 'nudges' that build up a junior colleague over time.

title: The Authentic Leader's Legacy
carnegie_principle: Arouse in the other person an eager want.
modern_context: The final capstone. Defining your own leadership philosophy.
practice_prompt: Summarize your core leadership values and how you will apply them to your next decade of work.

title: The 3-Sentence Slack Message
carnegie_principle: Respect your reader's attention. A Slack message should deliver one purpose, one context, and one ask — no more.
modern_context: Slack channels are firehoses. A tight message gets read; a wall of text gets skimmed or ignored.
practice_prompt: Read aloud a 3-sentence Slack message asking your manager to review a design doc by Friday. Keep it warm but tight.

title: The Email That Doesn't Need a Meeting
carnegie_principle: Write async when a meeting isn't needed. Lead with the decision or the ask, then provide the reasoning.
modern_context: Meeting fatigue is real. A well-structured email saves everyone thirty minutes and gives them time to think.
practice_prompt: Read aloud an email to your team proposing you switch weekly status meetings to async written updates. Open with the ask, back it up with one reason.
success_criteria: Ask appears in the first sentence

title: Status Updates That Travel
carnegie_principle: Write every update assuming a stakeholder three levels up will only read the first line. Make that line carry the value.
modern_context: Your manager forwards your update. Their manager skims it. Structure compounds across the chain.
practice_prompt: Give a 60-second verbal weekly update on a fictional project called Orbit Launch. Headline result first, then brief context, then the single risk you want help with.
success_criteria: Headline result in the first sentence
Exactly one risk, not a list

title: Loom in 90 Seconds
carnegie_principle: An async video needs the same shape as a live pitch: a hook, one insight, and a clear ask.
modern_context: Loom and similar tools let you replace meetings, but a 10-minute ramble does more damage than a 3-line email.
practice_prompt: Record a 90-second voice pitch to your boss for attending a conference. Hook in the first 10 seconds, one concrete insight, a clear ask with a date.
success_criteria: Hook lands in the first 10 seconds
Explicit ask tied to a date

title: The Good-Enough Slack Reply
carnegie_principle: Most async pings need an acknowledgement, not a solution. Two words of warmth buys your focus back.
modern_context: Every Slack ping pulls you out of flow. You can honor the sender without stopping your own work.
practice_prompt: A teammate pings you at 9 AM asking you to review a pull request today. Record a 20-second spoken reply that acknowledges without committing to timing yet.
success_criteria: Acknowledges the sender by name or intent

title: Observation vs. Judgement
carnegie_principle: Separate observation from evaluation. 'You're always late' is judgement; 'You arrived at 10:15 for a 10:00 meeting' is observation.
modern_context: Judgement triggers defense; observation invites conversation. The same conflict resolves twice as fast when you lead with facts.
practice_prompt: Record 45 seconds of feedback for a teammate who missed a deadline. Lead with pure observation — what happened, no labels — and end with one specific request.
success_criteria: Ends with a clear request, not a complaint

title: Challenge Directly, Care Personally
carnegie_principle: Radical Candor means caring personally AND challenging directly. Avoiding hard truths isn't kindness, it's ruinous empathy.
modern_context: In flat, remote teams, indirect feedback gets misread. Directness paired with warmth is the only thing that actually changes behavior.
practice_prompt: A report of yours is under-performing. Record a 60-second one-on-one opener that is both direct about the gap AND warm about the person. Avoid sandwiching.
success_criteria: Frames the problem as shared, not adversarial

title: Mirrors and Labels
carnegie_principle: Mirror the last few words they said. Label the feeling you're hearing. Then stop talking and watch the conversation open up.
modern_context: Chris Voss negotiated hostage releases with this technique. It works on angry customers, a stuck stakeholder, or your own teenager.
practice_prompt: A colleague says: 'I'm honestly at my limit with this project.' Record your 30-second reply using one mirror and one label. Offer no solution, no fix.
success_criteria: Mirrors the last few meaningful words
Labels a feeling using 'it sounds like...'

title: The Apology That Lands
carnegie_principle: A real apology names the specific harm, takes ownership, and commits to a change — without a single 'but'.
modern_context: 'I'm sorry you feel that way' is anti-apology. The real thing is shorter, harder, and repairs the relationship.
practice_prompt: You sent a Slack message that came across sharper than intended. Record a 30-second verbal apology to the recipient. No 'but'. No 'you took it the wrong way'.
success_criteria: Names the specific message or moment

title: Situation, Behavior, Impact
carnegie_principle: Feedback lands when it's tied to a specific moment, a specific action, and a specific consequence — and nothing more.
modern_context: Vague praise and vague criticism both fail. Only specificity changes behavior, in either direction.
practice_prompt: A teammate just presented well in a meeting. Record 30 seconds of positive SBI feedback — the situation, exactly what they did, and what impact it had.
success_criteria: One specific behavior, not a personality trait
One concrete impact on the meeting or audience

title: Saying No With Grace
carnegie_principle: A graceful no honors both the ask and the relationship. Acknowledge, decline cleanly, offer what you can do.
modern_context: Over-committing is the fastest path to burnout and broken promises. A clean no protects everyone.
practice_prompt: Your manager asks you to lead a new project on top of your current load. Record a 45-second verbal reply that declines respectfully and offers one concrete alternative.
success_criteria: Acknowledges the ask without dismissing it
Clear no with a brief, honest reason
Offers one specific alternative you can commit to

title: Receiving Criticism Without Defending
carnegie_principle: Three seconds of silence. Paraphrase what you heard. Ask one clarifying question. Defend later — if at all.
modern_context: The instinct to justify is the fastest way to turn a learning moment into an argument and lose both.
practice_prompt: Imagine your manager just said 'Your Q1 work has been sloppier than Q4.' Record a 45-second verbal reply that doesn't defend — paraphrase, ask, then commit.
success_criteria: Paraphrases what was said in your own words

title: De-Escalating a Heated Message
carnegie_principle: Switch modalities. A hot Slack thread cools the moment one person says 'let's jump on voice for five minutes.'
modern_context: Text is lossy. Tone and timing get lost, and a misread word compounds. Moving to voice resets the social contract.
practice_prompt: A teammate sends: 'this is completely broken and you didn't even test it.' Record a 30-second verbal reply that de-escalates and proposes a short voice call.
success_criteria: Tone does not match their heat
Proposes a voice call with a specific time window

title: Negotiating a Raise in One Minute
carnegie_principle: Anchor high. Back the number with outcomes, not effort. Silence after the ask is your strongest tool.
modern_context: Most raise conversations die in the first 30 seconds because people negotiate effort. Outcomes are what move the number.
practice_prompt: Imagine your annual review. Record a 60-second verbal ask for a 15-percent raise — anchor with the number, back it with one concrete outcome, and stop talking.
success_criteria: Supports the number with a measurable outcome, not hours worked
Ends with deliberate silence, not a hedging sentence

title: Cross-Cultural Directness
carnegie_principle: The same message reads differently across cultures. Adjust the wrapping, keep the content.
modern_context: Erin Meyer's Culture Map: the Dutch find Americans vague; the Japanese find them rude. You need both dials to work across a global team.
practice_prompt: You need to tell a teammate their deliverable missed the mark. Record two 30-second versions back to back: one for a direct-culture colleague, then one for an indirect-culture colleague.
success_criteria: First version is explicit and fast
Second version opens with context or relationship before the issue

title: The Calibrated Question
carnegie_principle: A calibrated question starts with 'What' or 'How'. It forces your counterpart to think and collaborate. It never has a yes/no answer.
modern_context: Chris Voss used calibrated questions to resolve hostage crises. They work equally well to unstick a stalled project, a resistant stakeholder, or a budget conversation.
practice_prompt: Your project timeline just got cut in half by a VP. Record a 45-second response that uses only calibrated questions — no statements, no complaints. Try: 'How am I supposed to do that?' and 'What's driving the new deadline?'
success_criteria: Uses at least two 'What' or 'How' questions

title: The Accusation Audit
carnegie_principle: List every negative thing your counterpart could be thinking about you before they say it. Naming the fear removes its power.
modern_context: Opening a difficult conversation by naming the worst the other person might think defuses defensiveness instantly. It signals confidence and empathy simultaneously.
practice_prompt: You're about to ask your manager for a month of remote work. Record a 60-second opener using an accusation audit — name every objection they might have before making the ask.
success_criteria: Names at least two genuine objections proactively
Transitions smoothly from audit to ask

title: The Late-Night FM DJ Voice
carnegie_principle: Slow down. Drop your register. A calm, deep, measured voice communicates control and safety — the opposite of urgency and pressure.
modern_context: Voice tone accounts for 38% of the message received. When a conversation heats up, the person who slows down wins the emotional frame.
practice_prompt: Re-read this sentence twice: 'I want to make sure we find something that works for both of us.' First at your normal pace and pitch. Then deliberately slower, lower, and with a pause after 'sure'.
success_criteria: Pitch drops noticeably in second version

title: No-Oriented Questions
carnegie_principle: Asking for a 'no' feels safer than asking for a 'yes'. People commit more freely to something they've chosen to reject than something they've been pushed toward.
modern_context: Instead of 'Can we get started on this?' try 'Is now a bad time?' The second version removes pressure and the 'no' response actually opens the door.
practice_prompt: You need a colleague to review your proposal by Thursday. Record a 30-second ask using only no-oriented framing. You cannot use 'can you', 'would you', or 'could you'.
success_criteria: Question is framed toward a 'no' answer

title: Getting to 'That's Right'
carnegie_principle: Summarise their position so accurately and completely that they say 'that's right' — not 'you're right'. The first signals understanding; the second means they've given up.
modern_context: In any negotiation, the moment your counterpart says 'that's right' is the moment real progress begins. It confirms they feel heard.
practice_prompt: An AI colleague says: 'I'm frustrated because I keep picking up work others drop, and no one seems to notice or care.' Record a 45-second summary that earns a 'that's right'. Use a label and a paraphrase together.
success_criteria: Paraphrases both the facts and the emotion
Ends at silence — no proposed solution

title: Tactical Empathy Under Pressure
carnegie_principle: Tactical empathy is understanding and articulating the other side's point of view — not agreeing with it. It deactivates hostility without surrendering your position.
modern_context: When a client is furious, the instinct to explain or defend makes it worse. Labelling their emotion first buys time, respect, and a window to problem-solve.
practice_prompt: An angry client says on a call: 'Your team has wasted three weeks and we have nothing to show for it. I'm done waiting.' Record a 45-second response using tactical empathy — no defence, no counter-narrative.
success_criteria: Does not apologise for something you haven't validated yet

title: The Ackermann Model
carnegie_principle: Start at 65% of your target. Move to 85%, then 95%, then 100% — with decreasing increments and a non-monetary item thrown in at the end. The math signals a real limit.
modern_context: Experienced negotiators recognise round numbers as opening positions. Odd, precise numbers signal a real bottom line. The Ackermann Model builds in that psychology.
practice_prompt: You want $120k for a contract. Your opening is $78k. Walk through the Ackermann sequence aloud, narrating each move: what you offer, why the decrements signal finality, and what you add at the end.
success_criteria: Four moves at approximately 65/85/95/100 of target
Each increment is smaller than the last
Final offer includes a non-monetary concession

title: Finding Black Swans
carnegie_principle: Every negotiation has pieces of information that, if revealed, would change everything. Black Swans are unknown unknowns. Your job is to surface them.
modern_context: In business deals, the real constraint is rarely what's stated — budget, timeline, authority. The black swan is usually a hidden fear, an undisclosed constraint, or a relationship.
practice_prompt: You're negotiating a vendor contract. The other side keeps saying 'the budget is fixed.' Record a 60-second sequence of three calibrated questions designed to uncover what's really driving that constraint.
success_criteria: Questions probe motivation, not just information

title: Deadline as a Tool
carnegie_principle: Deadlines are almost always more flexible than they appear — especially your counterpart's. Disclosing your own deadline is rarely the disaster people fear.
modern_context: 80% of concessions happen in the last 20% of the time available. Understanding who owns the deadline and why reframes the entire negotiation.
practice_prompt: A vendor says they need your decision by end of week. Record a 45-second response that probes the deadline without dismissing it — use a calibrated question and a label.
success_criteria: Does not accept the deadline as fixed

title: Silence After the Ask
carnegie_principle: After you make an ask — especially a number — stop talking. Every word after the ask weakens it.
modern_context: The silence after a salary number or a project proposal is where most people break and start negotiating against themselves. Stillness is a power signal.
practice_prompt: Say this sentence and then hold silence for 5 full seconds: 'Based on everything I've outlined, I'd need $145,000 to make this work.' Record it. Don't laugh, fill, or add qualifiers.
success_criteria: Clear, specific ask in one sentence
Silence holds for at least 3 seconds

title: Labelling Negative Emotions
carnegie_principle: Labelling a negative feeling diminishes it. Ignoring it amplifies it. The label doesn't have to be perfect — attempting it is what matters.
modern_context: Naming a colleague's frustration, anxiety, or reluctance before they voice it builds trust and shortens the path to agreement.
practice_prompt: Record a 45-second response to this: 'I just don't know if this is the right direction.' Use two labels — one for the emotion you detect, one for the uncertainty underneath it. Start each with 'It sounds like...'
success_criteria: Labels use tentative language, not assertions
Does not pivot to advice or solutions

title: Bending Reality with Loss Framing
carnegie_principle: People are more motivated by avoiding loss than by achieving gain. Frame your offer in terms of what they lose by not acting.
modern_context: Kahneman's prospect theory: a loss of $100 feels twice as painful as a gain of $100 feels good. Ethical loss framing isn't manipulation — it's helping people see the full picture.
practice_prompt: You're pitching a training programme. Record two 30-second versions of the same pitch: one gain-framed ('you'll improve your team's output'), one loss-framed. The content must be identical.

title: The 'How Am I Supposed to Do That?' Pivot
carnegie_principle: When faced with an unreasonable demand, a calm calibrated question — not a refusal — keeps the conversation open and shifts the problem back to them.
modern_context: Saying 'no' or 'that's impossible' escalates. A genuine, curious 'how am I supposed to do that?' makes the other person solve the problem they just created.
practice_prompt: Your client just asked you to deliver in half the time for the same price. Record a 30-second response built entirely around 'How am I supposed to do that?' — calm, not sarcastic.
success_criteria: Uses the exact phrase or a close variant
Tone is genuinely curious, not passive-aggressive
Does not offer a concession or counter-offer

title: Navigating the First 'No'
carnegie_principle: 'No' is not the end — it's the beginning of the real negotiation. It signals discomfort, not a final position. Label it and keep going.
modern_context: Most deals die because one side hears 'no' and retreats. 'No' usually means 'I'm not comfortable' or 'I don't understand yet'.
practice_prompt: A prospect says 'I don't think we have the budget for this right now.' Record a 45-second response that treats this as a 'no' signal, not a final answer — label it, and use a calibrated question to move forward.
success_criteria: Does not immediately pivot to a discount
Acknowledges the concern with a label
Uses a calibrated question to explore the real objection

title: The Negotiation Post-Mortem
carnegie_principle: Every negotiation is a data set. What signals did you miss? What move would have changed the outcome? Deliberate debrief is where skill compounds.
modern_context: Elite negotiators replay the conversation. Amateurs move on. The debrief is the most underpractised skill in the toolkit.
practice_prompt: Think of a recent negotiation — salary, contract, project scope — that didn't go your way. Narrate a 90-second debrief: what was your opening, what signals did you miss, and what one move would you change?
success_criteria: Names a specific moment where the outcome shifted

title: Reciprocity — Give First
carnegie_principle: Give something of genuine value before you ask for anything. Reciprocity is hardwired — an unrequested gift creates a felt obligation to return it.
modern_context: Cialdini's research shows that even small, unexpected gifts dramatically increase compliance. In business, value-first content, introductions, and insights all activate the same mechanism.
practice_prompt: You want a senior leader's time for a 30-minute coffee chat. Record a 60-second voicemail where you lead with something genuinely useful to them before making the ask.
success_criteria: The gift is specific and relevant to the recipient
Ask comes last and is clear

title: Reciprocity — The Unexpected Favour
carnegie_principle: Uninvited help is more powerful than a favour offered in exchange. The element of surprise amplifies the felt obligation.
modern_context: Forwarding a relevant article, making an unsolicited introduction, or flagging a risk in someone else's project are all high-ROI reciprocity triggers.
practice_prompt: Record a 45-second Slack voice note to a colleague you want a favour from next month. You're sending it today — two weeks before the ask — with a piece of information that's useful specifically to their work.
success_criteria: No ask anywhere in the message
The gift is personalised, not generic

title: Scarcity — Authentic Urgency
carnegie_principle: Scarcity increases perceived value. But only genuine scarcity works — people are now finely tuned to fake countdown timers and manufactured limits.
modern_context: Authentic scarcity means communicating real constraints: limited capacity, a genuine deadline, or exclusive access that truly is limited.
practice_prompt: You have three consulting spots open and genuinely want to fill them this quarter. Record a 60-second pitch that communicates scarcity without any manufactured urgency. Every constraint you mention must be real.
success_criteria: Communicates the genuine cost of waiting

title: Authority — Borrowed Credibility
carnegie_principle: Authority transfers. Citing a credible source, a prestigious client, or an expert's endorsement borrows their credibility for your message.
modern_context: In a world of information overload, credibility signals — case studies, expert quotes, logos — act as decision shortcuts for busy people.
practice_prompt: Pitch a new internal tool to your leadership team. Record a 60-second version that borrows authority from at least two external sources — a study, a known company, or an expert. Make it natural, not a citation list.
success_criteria: Sources feel relevant, not dropped in
Doesn't lead with credentials — uses them to support a point

title: Authority — Establish Your Own
carnegie_principle: Self-authority is built before the meeting, not during it. Titles, track records, published thinking, and confident framing all pre-load the room.
modern_context: Walking into a room where people already trust you compounds everything else. LinkedIn, a talk, a case study — these are authority-building assets, not vanity.
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second professional bio in third person, as if introducing yourself at a conference. Focus on outcomes and proof points, not job titles or time served.
success_criteria: At least two outcome-based proof points

title: Social Proof — Numbers That Persuade
carnegie_principle: People follow people. A specific number of users, customers, or believers is one of the strongest low-effort persuasion signals available.
modern_context: Social proof short-circuits individual evaluation. '1,200 teams use this' does more persuasive work than a five-paragraph feature list.
practice_prompt: You're pitching a product to a new prospect. Record a 60-second segment that weaves in three social proof signals naturally — numbers, well-known names, or categories of users. They should feel like context, not bragging.
success_criteria: At least one is a specific number

title: Liking — Genuine Common Ground
carnegie_principle: We buy from, vote for, and follow people we like. Liking is built on similarity, genuine interest, and warmth — not charm performance.
modern_context: Finding real common ground before a business conversation shortens every subsequent step. Shared experience, shared frustration, or shared vision all create liking.
practice_prompt: Before a mock sales call, you discover the prospect went to the same university and is also a runner. Record a 60-second opening that uses both touchpoints naturally — without it feeling like research you're reporting back.
success_criteria: Feels organic, not recited from a dossier

title: Commitment and Consistency — The Small Yes
carnegie_principle: A small public commitment creates psychological pressure to stay consistent with it. The foot-in-the-door technique works because people want to see themselves as consistent.
modern_context: Getting a prospect to agree to a small, low-stakes action — a 15-minute call, a quick review, a single question answered — dramatically increases the odds of a larger yes later.
practice_prompt: You want a senior leader to champion your project. Record a 60-second ask for the smallest possible commitment — something so easy to say yes to that refusal would feel unreasonable.
success_criteria: The ask is genuinely small and low-effort
Frames the small commitment as part of a larger journey

title: Commitment — Getting to Public Commitment
carnegie_principle: Public commitments are stronger than private ones. Written commitments are stronger still. The act of declaring in front of others creates accountability.
modern_context: In a team meeting, a 'sounds good' is weaker than 'I'll send that by Thursday'. Getting spoken or written commitments in front of peers hardens the agreement.
practice_prompt: You're wrapping up a project meeting. Two action items were agreed verbally but no one has said them out loud clearly. Record a 45-second meeting close that surfaces both commitments and gets each owner to name their own deadline.
success_criteria: Each owner states their own deadline
You narrate it, not dictate it

title: Unity — The We Frame
carnegie_principle: Unity is the deepest influence principle: shared identity. We help, trust, and follow people we consider part of our 'we' — family, tribe, community, mission.
modern_context: In sales and leadership, shifting from 'I'm selling to you' to 'we're solving this together' changes the relational frame entirely. It's not linguistic trick — it must be genuine.
practice_prompt: You're opening a difficult conversation about restructuring with your team. Record a 60-second opener that establishes shared identity and shared stakes before revealing the news. 'We' should appear at least four times, but only where it's authentic.
success_criteria: At least four authentic uses of 'we'
Shared identity is named (team, mission, or shared challenge)

title: Unity — Shared Enemy, Shared Goal
carnegie_principle: A common challenge or adversary unites people faster than shared success. The enemy can be a market condition, a competitor, or an internal problem — never a person.
modern_context: Rallying a team around a competitor's rising market share, a tightening deadline, or a customer problem they all feel works because it activates tribal cohesion.
practice_prompt: Your team is fragmented on priorities. Record a 60-second all-hands opener that unites them around a shared external challenge. The enemy cannot be a person or another team.
success_criteria: Shared challenge is external, not internal
Ends with a unifying call to action

title: Reading Influence Attempts
carnegie_principle: The best defence against manipulation is recognising the principles being used against you. Naming the technique deactivates it.
modern_context: Salespeople, recruiters, and negotiators use Cialdini principles deliberately. Spotting them in real time lets you pause, evaluate, and respond rather than react.
practice_prompt: Listen to this pitch: 'We only have two spots left this quarter [scarcity], and the last three clients in your space saw a 30% uplift [social proof]. Most people in your position get started with a quick call [commitment].' Record a 60-second analysis naming each principle used and how you'd respond to each one.
success_criteria: Proposes a specific counter-move for each

title: Ethical Influence — Where the Line Is
carnegie_principle: Influence principles are tools. The ethics depend entirely on whether you're helping someone make a better decision or overriding their judgement for your benefit.
modern_context: Manufactured scarcity, fake social proof, and weaponised reciprocity are short-term wins and long-term brand destruction. Authentic influence compounds.
practice_prompt: Describe a real situation from your work where you could use a Cialdini principle either ethically or unethically. Record a 60-second reflection on where the line is in that specific case and what the ethical version looks like.
success_criteria: Describes both the ethical and unethical version

title: Observations Without Evaluation
carnegie_principle: Separate what you observe from what you interpret. An observation is a recording device; an evaluation is an editorial. Only the first invites dialogue.
modern_context: Marshall Rosenberg's Nonviolent Communication begins here. 'You're always disorganised' shuts down; 'The report was missing three sections' opens a conversation.
practice_prompt: A colleague is consistently late to your team's morning standup. Record a 45-second feedback opener using pure observation — no adjectives that evaluate, no frequency exaggerations like 'always' or 'never'.
success_criteria: At least one verifiable, time-stamped fact

title: The Feelings Vocabulary
carnegie_principle: There is a difference between 'I feel like you don't care' (a thought about someone) and 'I feel worried' (an emotion you own). Only the second is an NVC feeling.
modern_context: Most people have a feelings vocabulary of about fifteen words. Expanding it increases emotional precision, reduces misunderstanding, and dramatically improves conflict resolution.
practice_prompt: Think of a difficult professional situation from the past week. Record a 45-second reflection using at least three distinct NVC feeling words — not interpretations like 'disrespected' or 'manipulated', but actual emotions like 'anxious', 'frustrated', or 'disappointed'.
success_criteria: Feelings are owned, not attributed to others

title: Needs vs. Strategies
carnegie_principle: Behind every request is a need. Confusing the strategy (what you want) with the need (why you want it) locks conflict in place. Surface the need and multiple strategies become available.
modern_context: In a team conflict about remote vs. office work, the surface strategies differ but the underlying needs — autonomy, connection, focus — might be entirely compatible.
practice_prompt: A colleague refuses to use the new project management tool you've chosen. Record a 60-second NVC analysis: what strategy are they defending, what underlying need might be driving it, and what alternative strategies could meet both your needs?
success_criteria: Distinguishes clearly between strategy and need
Names the colleague's underlying need charitably
Proposes at least one alternative that meets both needs

title: Making Requests, Not Demands
carnegie_principle: A request leaves the other person genuinely free to say no. A demand — even a politely worded one — carries a consequence for refusal. The difference is in your internal posture, not the words.
modern_context: Requests build collaborative relationships. Demands, even subtle ones, breed compliance and resentment. The test: how do you respond when they say no?
practice_prompt: You need your direct report to re-do a piece of work that doesn't meet the standard. Record a 45-second request — not a demand — that includes what you need, why it matters, and leaves them genuinely free to negotiate the timeline.

title: Empathy as Full Presence
carnegie_principle: Empathy is not agreement. It is not advice. It is not sharing a similar story. It is being fully present with another person's experience without trying to fix or explain it.
modern_context: In high-pressure workplaces, the instinct is to problem-solve. But a colleague who feels heard is more likely to solve their own problem than one who received your ten-step action plan.
practice_prompt: A teammate says: 'I don't know if I'm cut out for this. Everyone else seems to get it and I'm just drowning.' Record a 45-second empathetic response — no advice, no reassurance, no story about when you felt the same way.
success_criteria: Names what you're hearing using feeling and need

title: NVC in a Heated Conflict
carnegie_principle: In conflict, both parties are trying to meet legitimate needs through incompatible strategies. NVC's job is to make the needs visible so new strategies become possible.
modern_context: Most workplace conflicts aren't about the stated issue — a process, a deadline, a tool. They're about unmet needs for recognition, autonomy, or fairness.
practice_prompt: Two team members are in a dispute about who owns a project decision. Record a 90-second mediation opener using NVC: name what each side observes, what each side feels, and what underlying need each is trying to meet.
success_criteria: Observation, feeling, and need named for each party
No blame attributed to either side
Ends with a question that opens a new strategy space

title: Self-Empathy Before the Hard Conversation
carnegie_principle: You cannot give from an empty cup. Working out your own observation, feelings, and needs before a difficult conversation reduces reactivity and increases clarity.
modern_context: Pre-conversation NVC journalling — even 3 minutes — dramatically improves outcomes. You enter the room knowing what you actually need, not just what you're upset about.
practice_prompt: You're about to give critical feedback to a colleague who has let you down repeatedly. Record a 60-second self-empathy prep: what did you observe, what are you feeling, and what need of yours isn't being met?
success_criteria: Need is universal (fairness, reliability, collaboration) not strategic (I want them to stop)

title: NVC in Performance Reviews
carnegie_principle: A performance review delivered in NVC is specific, ownable, and actionable. One delivered in evaluation and judgement is defended against, not heard.
modern_context: HR research consistently shows that employees retain almost nothing from critical feedback delivered judgementally. The structure matters as much as the content.
practice_prompt: Give a 90-second mock performance review for a team member who is technically strong but misses deadlines. Use the four NVC components: observation, feeling, need, request.
success_criteria: No character assessments — only specific behaviours
Ends with a clear, negotiable request

title: The Hook — First 8 Seconds
carnegie_principle: The first 8 seconds of any talk, pitch, or presentation decide whether the audience lends you their attention or begins mentally checking out.
modern_context: Audiences grant attention provisionally. A strong hook — a counterintuitive claim, a specific number, a 5-word scene — signals that the next few minutes are worth their time.
practice_prompt: You are opening a presentation on why your company should enter a new market. Record only the first 8 seconds — your hook. No 'good mornings', no 'today I'll be covering'. Start in the middle of something true and interesting.

title: And / But / Therefore
carnegie_principle: Every story worth telling has a tension structure: something was happening (AND), but something changed or challenged it (BUT), and that forced a response (THEREFORE).
modern_context: South Park creators Trey Parker and Matt Stone cut any scene that connects with 'and then'. The ABT structure forces causality and conflict — the two things that make people listen.
practice_prompt: Describe a project you worked on using only the ABT structure. Record it in 60 seconds. You must use the exact words 'and', 'but', and 'therefore' as structural hinges, not just incidentally.

title: Vulnerability as Strength
carnegie_principle: A speaker who shares a real failure or moment of doubt creates more trust than one who projects only competence. Vulnerability signals that you're not hiding anything.
modern_context: Brené Brown's research is clear: audiences connect through imperfection, not polish. In a business context, a calculated moment of vulnerability is one of the most persuasive moves available.
practice_prompt: Tell a 60-second professional story where you were wrong or failed. The failure should be real, specific, and relevant to something you're now better at. Don't explain it away — let it sit for a moment.
success_criteria: No immediate pivot to silver lining
Connects the failure to current capability

title: Data Storytelling
carnegie_principle: A data point without a story is noise. A story without data is anecdote. The combination — where the number illuminates the human moment — is persuasion.
modern_context: Executives forget slides. They remember the moment you said 'that number represents 40,000 people who couldn't get through to us last quarter'. The humanised number sticks.
practice_prompt: You have one data point: customer support wait times increased 40% last quarter. Record a 60-second story that makes a room of executives feel the weight of that number. Don't just say it — show it.
success_criteria: The number appears in a human context
Emotional consequence of the number is felt

title: The Pixar Story Spine
carnegie_principle: Once upon a time... Every day... Until one day... Because of that... Until finally... Ever since then. This six-beat spine forces story structure and is used by every Pixar film.
modern_context: The Pixar spine works for investor pitches, team retrospectives, and case studies. It forces you to identify the inciting incident and the transformation — the two things audiences actually care about.
practice_prompt: Use the six-beat Pixar spine to tell the founding story of your current project or company in 90 seconds. Each beat must be present. Use the exact phrase to mark each beat.
success_criteria: Inciting incident ('until one day') is specific and vivid
Transformation ('ever since then') is concrete and measurable

title: The Personal Brand Story
carnegie_principle: Your personal brand is not your job title. It is the story of why you do what you do, what you've overcome to do it, and what specifically you see that others miss.
modern_context: In a world where credentials are cheap and LinkedIn profiles look identical, story is the differentiator. A 90-second authentic origin story is worth fifty bullet points.
practice_prompt: Record your personal brand story in 90 seconds. Not your CV in reverse. The moment that changed your direction, what you saw, and where it's pointing. Someone should understand what makes you different by the end.
success_criteria: Names a unique perspective you carry
Ends with where you're going, not just where you've been

title: The Micro-Story
carnegie_principle: A story under 60 seconds forces ruthless edit. Every detail must earn its place. The constraint produces clarity.
modern_context: The best presenters have five to ten 60-second stories that they've refined to the bone. Each one makes a single point. This is the most transferable storytelling skill in the toolkit.
practice_prompt: Tell a story that illustrates the principle 'preparation matters more than talent' in under 45 seconds. Every word must work. Cut until it bleeds, then cut one more thing.

title: Ethos — Building Credibility Before You Speak
carnegie_principle: Ethos is the credibility and character that the audience perceives in you before you make a single argument. It is earned before the room, not during it.
modern_context: Aristotle identified ethos as the most powerful of the three appeals. In practice, it means your reputation, your preparation, and your first thirty seconds all do persuasive work before your argument begins.
practice_prompt: Open a mock board presentation on a contentious topic — cutting a product line. Record a 60-second opening that builds ethos before you make any argument. Demonstrate you've done the work, you understand the stakes, and you respect the room.
success_criteria: Acknowledges the weight of the decision
No argument made in the first 60 seconds — only credibility established

title: Pathos — Moving Your Audience
carnegie_principle: Pathos is the emotional appeal. It is not manipulation — it is helping the audience feel the full weight of what is actually at stake. Logic informs; emotion moves.
modern_context: Decisions are made emotionally and justified rationally. A proposal that makes the abstract concrete — the person behind the policy, the team behind the number — activates pathos ethically.
practice_prompt: You're pitching investment in a customer support overhaul. Record a 60-second pathos section — one story about one customer that makes the problem feel real. No statistics in this segment. One person, one experience.
success_criteria: One specific person or case (real or composite)
Emotional consequence is shown, not told

title: Logos — The Logic of Persuasion
carnegie_principle: Logos is the rational appeal — evidence, structure, and sound reasoning. It gives the audience permission to feel what your pathos made them feel. The sequence is: ethos earns the hearing, pathos creates the want, logos justifies the decision.
modern_context: A pitch with only emotion is dismissed as spin. A pitch with only logic is forgotten. The sequence of ethos → pathos → logos is the most durable persuasion architecture in history.
practice_prompt: Give a 90-second pitch that uses all three appeals in sequence: 30 seconds of ethos, 30 of pathos, 30 of logos. Choose a real proposal from your work. Name each section as you transition.
success_criteria: All three appeals present and labelled
Each section is distinct in tone

title: The Rule of Three
carnegie_principle: Three points are memorable. Two feels incomplete. Four feels like a list. The rule of three is the most reliable structure in rhetoric.
modern_context: From 'Life, Liberty, and the Pursuit of Happiness' to 'Location, Location, Location', the tricolon is the most used and most recalled rhetorical structure.
practice_prompt: Make your case for a process change at work using exactly three arguments. Record it in 60 seconds. Each argument gets one sentence. The third should be the strongest.
success_criteria: Each argument is one distinct point, not a continuation of the previous

title: The Rhetorical Question
carnegie_principle: A rhetorical question creates a moment of internal agreement in the listener. It invites them to arrive at the conclusion themselves — which makes it theirs, not yours.
modern_context: Used correctly, a rhetorical question breaks the transmission model of communication and makes the audience participants. Overused, it becomes tic.
practice_prompt: Make a 60-second case for a significant change in your team — remote working, a new process, or a budget shift. Use exactly two rhetorical questions. Each must earn its place: don't ask a question whose answer isn't immediately obvious.
success_criteria: Both questions have obvious answers that support your point
Questions feel natural in the flow, not performative

title: Franklin's Persuasion Method
carnegie_principle: Benjamin Franklin never argued directly for his own positions. He framed disagreement as curiosity, used tentative language, and let others arrive at his conclusions. It worked for 80 years.
modern_context: Franklin's method: replace 'I believe X is wrong' with 'I wonder if there might be another way to look at this.' Remove the ego from the position and the position becomes much easier to accept.
practice_prompt: You think your team's approach to a problem is wrong. Record a 60-second challenge using Franklin's method — curious framing, tentative language, no direct assertion that you're right or they're wrong.
success_criteria: No direct assertion of being correct
Uses tentative language ('I wonder', 'it seems', 'could it be')
Ends with a question, not a conclusion

title: Chiasmus and Inversion
carnegie_principle: Chiasmus is the reversal of grammatical structure in successive phrases. 'Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country.' The inversion creates emphasis and memorability.
modern_context: A well-constructed chiasmus makes a point twice, mirrors it, and makes it unforgettable. It is the difference between a sentence that is remembered and one that is noted.
practice_prompt: Create and deliver three chiasmic statements relevant to your industry or work. Each should be genuinely insightful, not just a word-swap trick. Record all three with a deliberate pause between each.
success_criteria: Each contains a real insight, not just a clever reversal
Delivery includes a pause after each

title: Separate People from the Problem
carnegie_principle: The relationship and the problem are two separate things. Attacking the problem together is productive. Attacking the person destroys both the relationship and the outcome.
modern_context: Roger Fisher and William Ury's 'Getting to Yes' opens here. In every negotiation, there is the substantive issue and the human dynamic. Conflating them is the most common reason deals collapse.
practice_prompt: A project negotiation has become personal — the other side seems to be attacking you, not your proposal. Record a 60-second response that separates the relational heat from the substantive issue and redirects to the problem.
success_criteria: Names the problem as separate from the person

title: Positions vs. Interests
carnegie_principle: A position is what someone says they want. An interest is why they want it. Positions conflict; interests often align. The breakthrough is always at the level of interests.
modern_context: Two siblings fight over an orange — both want the whole thing. One wants the juice; the other wants the peel for a cake. The same dynamic plays out in every business negotiation.
practice_prompt: A client insists on a six-week delivery timeline and won't budge. Record a 60-second response that probes beneath the stated position to surface the underlying interest. Use calibrated questions only.
success_criteria: Does not argue with the stated position
Uses at least two questions aimed at the underlying why

title: Inventing Options for Mutual Gain
carnegie_principle: Most negotiations present two options: my way or yours. The job of principled negotiation is to invent a third option that neither side had thought of — one that meets both parties' interests.
modern_context: The creative phase of negotiation is deliberately separated from the evaluative phase. Brainstorming options without committing to any of them surfaces possibilities that positional bargaining never reaches.
practice_prompt: You and a partner disagree on how to split responsibilities for a new project. Record a 90-second brainstorm session — out loud — generating at least five options that could meet both your interests. No evaluation during the brainstorm.
success_criteria: At least five distinct options generated
No evaluation or rejection during generation
At least one option is genuinely creative — not a split-the-difference compromise

title: Objective Criteria
carnegie_principle: An agreement based on objective criteria — market rate, expert opinion, precedent, law — is more durable and more defensible than one based on who applied the most pressure.
modern_context: When two parties argue from positions, the stronger party usually wins. When both anchor to independent standards, the outcome is fair and the relationship survives.
practice_prompt: You're negotiating a fee for a consulting project and the client says your rate is too high. Record a 60-second response that introduces three objective criteria to anchor the conversation — without issuing an ultimatum.
success_criteria: Invites the other side to propose their own criteria

title: Know Your BATNA
carnegie_principle: Your Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement is your real source of power. The stronger your BATNA, the less you need this deal — and that changes everything about how you negotiate.
modern_context: Most people walk into negotiations without knowing their walkaway point. BATNA preparation is the single highest-leverage thing you can do before any negotiation begins.
practice_prompt: You are about to negotiate a job offer. Record a 60-second BATNA preparation monologue: what is your best alternative if this offer falls through, how strong is it, and how does knowing it change your opening position?
success_criteria: Strength of the BATNA is honestly assessed
Names at least one concrete change to negotiation approach that follows from the BATNA

title: Strengthening Your BATNA
carnegie_principle: You don't just discover your BATNA — you develop it. The more attractive your alternatives, the stronger your negotiating position in every conversation.
modern_context: Before a salary negotiation, apply for two other roles. Before a vendor renewal, get two competitor quotes. Improving your BATNA is a negotiation strategy that begins weeks before the meeting.
practice_prompt: Think of an upcoming negotiation — a contract renewal, a salary review, a partnership deal. Record a 60-second plan for how you would actively strengthen your BATNA before walking into the room.
success_criteria: Proposes two or more concrete BATNA-building actions

title: Negotiation Judo — Deflecting Attacks
carnegie_principle: When attacked, don't push back. Step to the side. Redirect the energy of the attack toward the problem. Negotiation judo turns aggression into shared problem-solving.
modern_context: A personal attack in a negotiation is almost always a sign of frustration, not a genuine character assessment. Treating it as information rather than an insult changes the dynamic.
practice_prompt: In a contract negotiation, the other party says: 'Frankly, the quality of your last project was disappointing and I'm not sure we should be paying your rates at all.' Record a 60-second negotiation judo response — don't defend, redirect.
success_criteria: Does not defend against the attack
Acknowledges the concern without conceding the point
Redirects to objective criteria or joint problem-solving

title: The Yesable Proposition
carnegie_principle: Structure your ask so the other party can say yes without losing face, violating their principles, or justifying to their constituents. Make it easy to agree.
modern_context: Negotiators who think only about what they want often make proposals that are technically acceptable but psychologically impossible to accept. The yesable proposition solves this.
practice_prompt: You need a competitor to agree to a data-sharing arrangement. Record a 60-second proposal that is structured so they can say yes easily — it meets their interests, respects their constraints, and gives them something to tell their own team.
success_criteria: Proposal explicitly accounts for their interests
Easy to say yes to without conditions

title: Negotiating When Power is Unequal
carnegie_principle: When the other party is more powerful, your job is not to win — it is to protect your interests, know your BATNA, and find objective criteria that limit their ability to simply impose a result.
modern_context: Negotiating with a much larger company, a domineering boss, or a monopoly supplier requires a different strategy. Power doesn't make a deal fair — objective criteria and a strong BATNA do.
practice_prompt: You are a small agency negotiating payment terms with a large enterprise client whose standard terms are 90 days. Record a 60-second response that doesn't capitulate or confront — it uses objective criteria and protects your interests.
success_criteria: References industry standard or objective criteria
Proposes a specific alternative without ultimatum

title: From Positions to Partnership
carnegie_principle: The goal of principled negotiation is not to win — it is to reach an agreement that is wise, efficient, and leaves the relationship intact or stronger.
modern_context: Deals between people who will work together again require a different success metric than one-off transactions. A 'win' that damages the relationship is often a long-term loss.
practice_prompt: Wrap up a mock negotiation with a vendor you'll work with for the next three years. Record a 90-second closing that confirms the agreement, acknowledges both sides' interests were met, and sets the tone for the ongoing relationship.
success_criteria: Acknowledges what the other side gained
Ends with a forward-looking relationship statement

title: What Makes a Conversation Crucial
carnegie_principle: A crucial conversation has three properties: high stakes, opposing opinions, and strong emotions. Most people either avoid them or handle them badly. Both outcomes damage results and relationships.
modern_context: Kerry Patterson's research found that the ability to handle crucial conversations is the single highest predictor of career success and relationship quality. The skill is learnable.
practice_prompt: Think of a crucial conversation you have been avoiding. Record a 60-second description: what makes it crucial (the three properties), what you've been doing instead of having it, and what the cost of continued avoidance is.
success_criteria: Names all three crucial conversation properties in this specific case
Articulates the cost of not having it

title: Start With Heart
carnegie_principle: Before entering a crucial conversation, ask yourself: what do I really want here? For myself, for the other person, for the relationship? Clarity of intent is the foundation.
modern_context: Most crucial conversations go wrong because the person entering them wants to win, not to solve. The moment you catch yourself trying to be right, you've lost the thread.
practice_prompt: You need to confront a peer who has been taking credit for your work. Before the conversation, record a 60-second 'Start With Heart' preparation: what do you want for yourself, for them, and for your working relationship? Not just what you're angry about.
success_criteria: Names a goal for self, other person, and relationship separately
Identifies one temptation to 'win' that you'll resist

title: Learn to Look — Spotting Safety Breaks
carnegie_principle: Watch for silence or violence. When people feel unsafe, they either go silent (withdraw, mask, avoid) or go violent (control, label, attack). Both signal that safety has broken down.
modern_context: Crucial Conversations research shows that dialogue stops the moment safety breaks — often before anyone notices. The skill of noticing these signals in real time is what separates effective communicators.
practice_prompt: Describe a recent meeting that went sideways. Record a 60-second retrospective analysis: at what point did safety break, what silence or violence signal appeared first, and what could you have done at that exact moment?
success_criteria: Names the specific moment safety broke
Identifies whether it was silence or violence
Proposes a specific intervention at that moment

title: Make It Safe — Mutual Purpose
carnegie_principle: Safety is restored when both parties believe you are working toward a common goal and that you care about their interests as well as your own. Establish mutual purpose explicitly.
modern_context: When someone suspects your motives, nothing you say will land. Naming the shared purpose first — before raising the difficult topic — creates the safety dialogue needs.
practice_prompt: You need to tell a high-performing employee that their interpersonal behaviour is damaging the team. Record a 60-second opening that establishes mutual purpose before raising the issue. The employee's interests must feel genuinely represented.
success_criteria: Mutual purpose named before the issue is raised
No issue introduced in the first 30 seconds

title: The Contrasting Statement
carnegie_principle: A contrasting statement addresses what you don't intend before stating what you do intend. It prevents the other person from catastrophising your purpose before they've heard you out.
modern_context: 'I don't want you to think I'm questioning your commitment — I do want to talk about the last three deadlines.' This structure neutralises the feared interpretation before it takes root.
practice_prompt: You need to raise a sensitive performance issue with a colleague who tends to become defensive. Record a 45-second opener using a contrasting statement. The 'don't' must address their most likely feared interpretation.
success_criteria: 'Don't' addresses the most feared interpretation, not a minor concern
Transition to the actual issue is smooth

title: Master My Stories
carnegie_principle: Between an event and an emotion there is always a story you told yourself. You can't control the event, but you can retell the story — and a different story produces a different emotional state.
modern_context: Patterson calls the self-told story the 'path to action'. Facts → Story → Feeling → Behaviour. Changing the story changes everything downstream.
practice_prompt: A colleague walked out of your presentation early without explanation. Record a 60-second 'Master My Stories' sequence: what story did you immediately tell yourself, what are two alternative stories that fit the same facts, and how does each story change your response?
success_criteria: Each story produces a different emotional and behavioural response

title: STATE Your Path
carnegie_principle: Share your facts. Tell your story. Ask for their path. Talk tentatively. Encourage testing. STATE is the structure for raising a difficult topic without triggering defensiveness.
modern_context: The STATE model separates observable facts from the story you've built around them — and keeps you honest about which is which. Most people lead with story and call it fact.
practice_prompt: A team member has missed three check-ins in a row. Use the STATE model to raise it. Record 90 seconds: start with the observable facts, share the story you've built, ask for their perspective, and stay tentative throughout.
success_criteria: Story framed as 'the conclusion I drew', not fact
Ends with a genuine question, not a rhetorical one

title: Explore Others' Paths
carnegie_principle: Ask, mirror, paraphrase, prime. After you've shared your view, your job is to genuinely understand theirs — especially when it contradicts yours.
modern_context: The natural impulse after sharing a difficult observation is to press for agreement. The crucial conversations move is to pause and invite their full version, without rebuttal.
practice_prompt: After raising a performance concern, the employee says: 'I think you're being unfair and I'm not the only one who feels that way.' Record a 60-second response that genuinely explores their path — no defence, no counter-evidence.
success_criteria: No defensive response to the 'unfair' claim
Uses at least one of: ask, mirror, paraphrase, prime
Creates space for their full version before any response

title: Move to Action
carnegie_principle: A crucial conversation without a clear decision and follow-up plan produces relief, not change. Who does what by when — said aloud and confirmed — is where dialogue becomes results.
modern_context: Most difficult conversations end with vague agreement and no accountability. The final step is converting shared understanding into documented, owned commitments.
practice_prompt: A difficult conversation about workload distribution has just reached resolution. Record a 60-second close that converts the agreement into action: who owns what, by when, and how you'll both know it's done.
success_criteria: Specific owner named for each action

title: The Crucial Conversation with Your Manager
carnegie_principle: Power dynamics don't change the model — they change the preparation. The crucial conversation with someone above you requires more careful mutual purpose work and more precise tentative language.
modern_context: Most career-limiting moments aren't caused by poor performance — they're caused by silence: things that needed to be said to a manager and weren't. Crucial conversations up the chain are a career skill.
practice_prompt: Your manager keeps overriding your decisions in front of the team without prior discussion. Record a 90-second crucial conversation opener — for a private meeting you've requested. Use mutual purpose, contrasting statement, and STATE.
success_criteria: Contrasting statement addresses the feared interpretation

title: The Big Idea
carnegie_principle: Every presentation must have a single sentence that captures the unique insight you're offering and stakes a position. If you can't say it in one sentence, you haven't found it yet.
modern_context: Nancy Duarte calls this the Big Idea: one sentence, one subject, one predicate, that states your unique point of view and the stakes for the audience if they don't act on it.
practice_prompt: Write and deliver the Big Idea sentence for a real presentation you need to give. It must contain your specific point of view, why it matters to this audience, and what changes if they act. Deliver it three times — tighten it each time.
success_criteria: Single sentence, not a phrase or a question
Specific point of view, not a topic
Third version is tighter than the first

title: The Presentation as Hero's Journey
carnegie_principle: The audience is the hero of your presentation — not you. Your job is to be the mentor: Yoda, not Luke. The audience's world must change by the end, not yours.
modern_context: Duarte's research across thousands of presentations shows that the most effective ones position the audience as the protagonist who will be transformed by adopting the speaker's idea.
practice_prompt: Redesign a presentation you've given before using the hero's journey frame. Record a 90-second narration of the new structure: what is the audience's current world, what is the world they could inhabit, and what is the call to adventure you're offering?
success_criteria: Audience positioned as hero, speaker as mentor
Current world and new world described specifically
Call to adventure is concrete and actionable

title: The Sparkline — Contrast as Structure
carnegie_principle: The most persuasive presentations oscillate between 'what is' and 'what could be'. Duarte calls this the sparkline. The gap between current reality and future possibility is where motivation lives.
modern_context: Analysis of Martin Luther King's 'I Have a Dream', Steve Jobs' iPhone launch, and Lincoln's Gettysburg Address all show the sparkline structure. It is the most reliably persuasive presentation architecture ever studied.
practice_prompt: Structure a 90-second segment of a pitch using the sparkline: alternate three times between a 'what is' statement (current painful reality) and a 'what could be' statement (desirable future). End on 'what could be'.
success_criteria: Three clear 'what is / what could be' alternations
The gap between current and future is emotionally felt, not just stated

title: Opening and Closing with Power
carnegie_principle: The opening and closing are the two highest-attention moments of any presentation. Most presenters waste both with process ('Today I'll cover three things') rather than substance.
modern_context: Duarte's rule: the opening must create immediate relevance or tension; the closing must issue a specific call to action that changes something for the audience.
practice_prompt: Record the opening and closing of a presentation on why your company should invest in AI tools. Each segment is 30 seconds. The opening creates tension or curiosity; the closing issues one specific, actionable ask.
success_criteria: Opening creates tension or curiosity in the first sentence
No process language in the opening
Closing contains a single specific call to action

title: Slide Design for Persuasion
carnegie_principle: A slide is a visual argument, not a transcript. Each slide makes one claim. The title is the claim. The visual is the evidence. If your title is a topic rather than a sentence, the slide has no argument.
modern_context: Duarte's research shows that audiences remember slides whose titles are full sentences making a claim — not topic labels. 'Q3 Revenue' is a label. 'Q3 Revenue Declined Because of APAC Churn' is an argument.
practice_prompt: Take three slides from a recent presentation of yours (or invent three). Rewrite each slide title as a full-sentence claim. Record a 60-second narration of the before and after for each, explaining what changed.
success_criteria: Each new title is a full sentence making a specific claim
Narration explains why the sentence version is stronger

title: Presenting to Executives
carnegie_principle: Executives want the recommendation first, the rationale second, and the detail only if asked. The pyramid structure inverts the academic essay and matches how decisions are actually made.
modern_context: Duarte and Barbara Minto's Pyramid Principle converge here: lead with the conclusion. Executives are pattern-matching for a decision, not following your narrative arc.
practice_prompt: Record a 90-second executive briefing on a decision that needs to be made. The recommendation must appear in the first sentence. Rationale follows. Detail is offered only at the end, conditionally.
success_criteria: Three supporting points for the recommendation
Detail offered as optional at the end

title: Rehearsal as Performance Design
carnegie_principle: Rehearsal is not repetition — it is the deliberate design of how you want the audience to feel at each moment. You rehearse the emotional arc, not just the words.
modern_context: Duarte's presentation coaching shows that the gap between adequate and excellent presentations is almost always rehearsal quality. Not word-perfect rehearsal — emotionally intentional rehearsal.
practice_prompt: Take a two-minute presentation segment and record it twice. First version: word-by-word rehearsal. Second version: annotate the emotional intent of each beat before recording — where should the audience feel tension, relief, curiosity? Compare the two deliveries.
success_criteria: Second version shows noticeable variation in pace, tone, and emphasis
At least one deliberate pause in the second version
Emotional intent described before recording the second version

title: Vulnerability-Based Trust
carnegie_principle: The only trust that enables real teamwork is vulnerability-based trust — the willingness to say 'I was wrong', 'I need help', and 'I'm sorry' without fear of political consequence.
modern_context: Patrick Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions of a Team begins here. Teams that lack vulnerability-based trust spend enormous energy managing impressions instead of solving problems.
practice_prompt: In a mock team retrospective, model vulnerability-based trust by being the first to name your own biggest contribution to a recent team failure. Record 60 seconds. No hedging, no blame-sharing, no silver lining.
success_criteria: Names your own specific contribution to the failure
Tone is matter-of-fact, not performatively self-flagellating

title: Mining for Conflict
carnegie_principle: Productive conflict is the pursuit of the best answer. Teams that avoid it produce watered-down decisions and artificial harmony. Leaders must actively mine for disagreement.
modern_context: Lencioni's research shows that teams with high trust can engage in productive conflict — passionate, unfiltered debate — and then fully commit to the outcome. Teams without trust can't do either.
practice_prompt: You're facilitating a strategy meeting where everyone is agreeing too quickly. Record a 60-second intervention that mines for conflict — surfaces the disagreement you suspect exists but nobody is voicing.
success_criteria: Names a specific assumption that could be wrong
Creates safety for dissent without undermining the process

title: Achieving Commitment
carnegie_principle: Commitment does not require consensus — it requires clarity and buy-in. A team can commit to a decision they disagree with, if the debate was genuine and the decision was clear.
modern_context: Lencioni distinguishes commitment from consensus: consensus means everyone agrees. Commitment means everyone is heard, the decision is clear, and everyone will execute it regardless of their preference.
practice_prompt: End a mock team meeting on a contentious decision — you've debated it, not everyone agrees. Record a 60-second close that achieves genuine commitment: summarises the decision, acknowledges the dissent, and gets explicit buy-in.
success_criteria: Explicit verbal commitment asked for and received

title: Peer Accountability
carnegie_principle: The most powerful form of accountability is peer-to-peer, not top-down. Teams that hold each other accountable to shared standards outperform teams that rely on the leader to do it.
modern_context: Lencioni's research shows that high-performing teams develop the norm of calling out underperformance, tardiness, and poor behaviour among themselves — without needing manager intervention.
practice_prompt: A peer on your team has been coasting for two weeks and it's affecting the team's results. Record a 60-second peer accountability conversation — not a complaint to the manager, a direct conversation with the peer.
success_criteria: Addressed directly, not via a third party
Framed as a team concern, not a personal attack

title: The Ideal Team Player
carnegie_principle: The ideal team player is hungry, humble, and smart. Hungry means driven and hardworking. Humble means no ego. Smart means emotionally intelligent in group settings.
modern_context: Lencioni argues that hiring and developing for all three traits simultaneously is the foundation of team performance. A person strong in two but weak in one is a recognisable archetype worth diagnosing.
practice_prompt: Think of a team you're currently part of. Record a 90-second analysis: where does the team sit on each of the three traits collectively, and what's the one weakness that most limits your collective performance?
success_criteria: All three traits assessed specifically for this team
One limiting weakness identified with evidence
Analysis is honest rather than diplomatic

title: Meetings That Work
carnegie_principle: Meetings are bad because they lack drama and context sensitivity. The solution is not fewer meetings — it is the right kind of meeting for the right purpose, with conflict built in.
modern_context: Lencioni proposes four meeting types: daily check-in (5 min), weekly tactical (45–90 min), monthly strategic (2–4 hrs), quarterly offsite. Mixing types in one meeting is the root of meeting dysfunction.
practice_prompt: Audit a recurring meeting you hate. Record a 60-second diagnosis using Lencioni's framework: what type of meeting is it trying to be, what type is it actually being, and what one structural change would fix it?
success_criteria: Names the intended and actual meeting type
Diagnosis is concrete, not vague ('we need better agendas')

title: Anchoring — The First Number Wins
carnegie_principle: The first number in any negotiation disproportionately influences the final outcome — even when it is arbitrary. This is anchoring. The person who sets the anchor shapes the range.
modern_context: Daniel Kahneman's research shows that even irrelevant numbers bias subsequent estimates. In pricing, salary, and contract discussions, anchoring first is one of the highest-leverage opening moves.
