practice_prompt: You're opening a fee negotiation for a project. Record a 45-second opening that anchors deliberately high, explains the anchor with logic, and leaves room to move — without sounding apologetic about the number.
success_criteria: Anchor is stated confidently, not tentatively
One logical rationale for the number provided

title: Framing Effects
carnegie_principle: The same fact, framed differently, produces different decisions. '10% chance of failure' and '90% chance of success' are identical — but they feel completely different.
modern_context: Kahneman's prospect theory shows that how information is presented systematically biases choices. Communicators who understand framing can present accurate information more persuasively.
practice_prompt: You're presenting a product success rate to a risk-averse board. Record two 30-second versions: one framed around failure risk, one framed around success probability. Same data, different frame. Then name which framing you'd use and why.
success_criteria: Rationale for chosen frame is strategic

title: Confirmation Bias in Feedback
carnegie_principle: We seek information that confirms what we already believe and discount information that contradicts it. Good decision-makers actively seek disconfirming evidence.
modern_context: In performance reviews, strategy sessions, and customer research, confirmation bias produces dangerously skewed data. Naming it and designing against it is a leadership competency.
practice_prompt: You believe a new product feature is a success. Record a 60-second team debrief that actively seeks disconfirming evidence — questions that could prove you wrong, data sources you haven't looked at, perspectives you've discounted.
success_criteria: At least three specific disconfirming questions asked
Names a data source or perspective not yet considered

title: The Planning Fallacy
carnegie_principle: We systematically underestimate how long things will take and how much they will cost — even when we have evidence from past projects to the contrary. This is the planning fallacy.
modern_context: Kahneman's antidote is the outside view: instead of planning from the inside ('how will this project go?'), consult the base rate ('how long do projects like this typically take?').
practice_prompt: You're estimating a three-month project timeline for stakeholders. Record a 60-second planning discussion that applies the outside view: reference at least two comparable past projects, apply a buffer rationale, and name the risks you're not accounting for.
success_criteria: At least one unaccounted risk named honestly

title: The Peak-End Rule in Presentations
carnegie_principle: People remember experiences by their most intense moment (the peak) and how they ended — not the average. Design your presentations, meetings, and conversations with this in mind.
modern_context: Kahneman's peak-end research has direct implications for how you structure presentations, client meetings, and difficult conversations. The last two minutes are disproportionately remembered.
practice_prompt: Redesign the ending of a presentation you've given. Record a 60-second analysis: what was the peak moment, how did it end previously, and what is the new ending designed to leave the audience with as a final impression?
success_criteria: New ending engineered for emotional memory

title: Overconfidence and How to Counter It
carnegie_principle: We are overconfident about our predictions, our knowledge, and our abilities — consistently and systematically. Calibrated uncertainty is a rare and valuable communication skill.
modern_context: Kahneman's research shows that experts are just as overconfident as non-experts in uncertain domains. Expressing calibrated confidence — 'I'm about 70% sure' — is more credible and more useful than false certainty.
practice_prompt: Give a 60-second update on a project with genuine uncertainty. Practice expressing calibrated confidence: use probability language, name what you don't know, and distinguish between what you're confident about and what you're guessing.
success_criteria: At least one specific probability or confidence level stated
Clear distinction between known and uncertain
Does not hedge everything — some claims stated with appropriate confidence

title: Slow Down the Fast Thinking
carnegie_principle: System 1 thinking is fast, intuitive, and often wrong in complex situations. System 2 is slow, deliberate, and effortful. High-stakes communication requires deliberately engaging System 2.
modern_context: In heated meetings, salary negotiations, and crisis communications, the instinct to respond immediately (System 1) almost always produces a worse outcome than pausing to think (System 2).
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second System 2 intervention technique for yourself: when you notice your System 1 taking over in a conversation, what is your specific physical or verbal cue to pause, what do you do in that pause, and how do you re-enter?
success_criteria: Re-entry strategy is specific, not just 'think before you speak'

title: Presence — What It Actually Is
carnegie_principle: Presence is not performance. It is the alignment between what you believe, what you feel, and what you express. When those three are in sync, other people feel it immediately.
modern_context: Amy Cuddy's research shows that presence — which she defines as being in the moment, enthusiastic, captivating — is detected by audiences within seconds and predicts interview outcomes, negotiation results, and leadership perception.
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second self-assessment of your presence: in what situations do you feel most aligned (belief, feeling, expression in sync)? In what situations does that alignment break down? What causes it to break?
success_criteria: Names a specific high-presence situation with detail
Names a specific low-presence situation with honesty
Identifies the specific trigger for breakdown

title: Warmth Before Competence
carnegie_principle: People first ask 'can I trust this person?' then 'can I respect their competence?' Establishing warmth before demonstrating competence is the sequence that earns both.
modern_context: Cuddy and colleagues' research across cultures shows that warmth judgements happen first and faster than competence judgements. Leading with competence while appearing cold is a reliable way to fail both tests.
practice_prompt: You're opening a job interview as the candidate. Record a 60-second opening that establishes warmth before demonstrating competence. The interviewer should feel you're genuinely interested in them before you tell them how good you are.
success_criteria: Warmth signals appear before competence signals
Genuine curiosity about the interviewer or role expressed
Competence established through a specific, relevant example — not a list

title: Managing Imposter Syndrome
carnegie_principle: Imposter syndrome is the feeling that you don't belong, that you've fooled everyone, and that you'll be exposed. The antidote is not thinking your way out — it is acting your way into a new self-perception.
modern_context: Cuddy's research shows that 'faking it till you become it' is not a cliché — it is a physiological process. Behaving as if you belong changes your hormone levels and your actual performance.
practice_prompt: Describe a high-stakes situation where you feel like an imposter. Record a 60-second preparation technique: what three behaviours would you adopt in that situation if you were confident you belonged? Commit to one for the next time.
success_criteria: One committed to explicitly for next occurrence

title: Power Posture and Physiology
carnegie_principle: Expansive, open postures signal dominance and ease across every culture studied. Contractive postures signal submission and anxiety. Your body primes your mind — not just others' perceptions of you.
modern_context: Cuddy's research and subsequent work show that body positioning affects cognitive performance, risk tolerance, and vocal authority. Before high-stakes conversations, physiology preparation matters.
practice_prompt: Record a 90-second voice note — standing in an expansive posture — pitching yourself for a promotion. Then record the same pitch sitting in a contracted posture. Compare your vocal authority, pace, and confidence in both versions.
success_criteria: Noticeably different vocal energy in the two versions
Honest assessment of which version sounds more credible

title: Nervous vs. Excited — Reframing Anxiety
carnegie_principle: 'I'm excited' and 'I'm nervous' are physiologically identical. The difference is only the story. Reappraising anxiety as excitement is one of the most evidence-based performance interventions available.
modern_context: Harvard research by Alison Wood Brooks shows that saying 'I'm excited' before a stressful performance — rather than trying to calm down — improves outcomes measurably. The arousal is an asset if reframed.
practice_prompt: Before your next high-stakes presentation or conversation, practise this reframe aloud. Record a 30-second statement: name the anxiety you feel, reframe it explicitly as excitement, and name the specific energy it gives you going in.
success_criteria: Energy named as a strategic asset for the specific situation

title: Authentic First Impressions
carnegie_principle: First impressions form in two seconds and are remarkably resistant to updating. The warmth-then-competence sequence determines whether the update favours or disadvantages you.
modern_context: Cuddy's research on first impressions in job interviews, investor pitches, and team introductions consistently shows that the two-second warmth signal determines whether subsequent competence evidence is absorbed positively or sceptically.
practice_prompt: Record your first 15 seconds of a professional introduction — as if meeting an important new client for the first time. No prepared monologue. Focus entirely on warmth, eye contact, and genuine curiosity. Review and re-record once.
success_criteria: Warmth is the dominant signal in the first 15 seconds
Second recording noticeably warmer than the first

title: Say Exactly What You Mean
carnegie_principle: Precision in language is a moral act. Vague speech produces vague thinking, and vague thinking produces bad outcomes. Saying what you mean exactly — even when it's hard — is a form of respect.
modern_context: Jordan Peterson draws on Solzhenitsyn and clinical psychology to argue that ideological and therapeutic language corrupts communication. In business, the same applies: jargon and euphemism obscure reality and delay solutions.
practice_prompt: Think of a situation at work where you or your team has been using vague language to avoid a difficult reality — a project is failing, a person isn't performing, a strategy isn't working. Record a 60-second statement that names the reality precisely, without softening or jargon.
success_criteria: The actual situation named in plain language
One specific consequence of continued vagueness identified

title: Clean Up Your Own House First
carnegie_principle: Before you criticise the world, your organisation, or your team, identify the things within your own control that you haven't done. Lead with self-examination.
modern_context: Peterson's Rule 6 — set your house in perfect order before you criticise the world — applies directly to leadership. The most credible critics are those who have visibly done the work on themselves first.
practice_prompt: You're about to raise a systemic problem in your organisation — a process that's broken, a culture that's toxic. Record a 60-second opening that leads with what you personally could have done better, before naming the systemic issue.
success_criteria: Personal accountability named first and specifically
Systemic issue raised after, not instead

title: Stand Up Straight — Presence as Posture
carnegie_principle: Posture signals status, confidence, and readiness — to yourself as much as to others. Slouching is a message your nervous system reads as defeat. Standing straight is a physiological choice with real consequences.
modern_context: Peterson's Rule 1 draws on dominance hierarchy research across species. In practice: the person who enters a room standing fully upright, making eye contact, moving deliberately, is read as higher-status before they say a word.
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second introduction of yourself — standing fully upright, shoulders back, direct eye contact with the camera, deliberate pace. Review it. Then record again, this time consciously amplifying each physical signal by 20%.
success_criteria: Second version shows measurable difference in physical presence

title: Pursue Meaning, Not Comfort
carnegie_principle: The most compelling communicators speak from a place of genuine conviction about something that matters. Comfort-seeking produces hedged, forgettable communication. Meaning-seeking produces the kind that moves people.
modern_context: Peterson argues that the antidote to anxiety is not safety — it is the voluntary confrontation of meaningful challenge. In communication terms: the speaker who is fighting for something real is impossible to ignore.
practice_prompt: Identify something in your professional life you genuinely believe matters and are willing to be unpopular for defending. Record a 60-second statement making that case — without hedging, without seeking approval, without softening the position.
success_criteria: Position is specific and staked clearly
Genuine conviction audible in the delivery

title: Articulate Your Assumptions
carnegie_principle: Most arguments are not about facts — they are about the unspoken assumptions beneath the facts. Surfacing your own assumptions is the most honest and effective way to advance any disagreement.
modern_context: Peterson's method in debate is to state his assumptions openly, invite challenge to them, and update when challenged with better reasoning. This is the opposite of persuasion-as-manipulation.
practice_prompt: You hold a strong position on a contested issue in your industry. Record a 90-second statement of that position that explicitly names the three key assumptions it rests on — and invites challenge to each one.
success_criteria: Genuine invitation to challenge each assumption

title: Listen to Understand, Not to Reply
carnegie_principle: Peterson's rule for conversation: summarise what the other person said well enough that they agree with your summary. Only then do you get to respond. This is the discipline that turns debate into dialogue.
modern_context: In business, the instinct in disagreement is to prepare your counter-argument while the other person is still speaking. Peterson's discipline — summarise first, respond second — is rare and disarming.
practice_prompt: After a colleague presents a position you disagree with, record a 60-second response that opens with a summary they would endorse — before you offer a single counter-point. The summary must be generous, not a straw man.
success_criteria: Summary is genuinely accurate and charitable
Other person's strongest point acknowledged, not their weakest

title: Asking for a Raise — The Full Sequence
carnegie_principle: A raise conversation is a negotiation, not a request for permission. It requires preparation, anchoring, outcome-framing, and the discipline to stay silent after the ask.
modern_context: Most people never ask for a raise, or ask apologetically and undermine their own position. The full sequence — preparation, anchor, rationale, silence — is a learnable skill that compounds over a career.
practice_prompt: Record the full raise conversation sequence in 90 seconds: open with the ask and anchor (specific number), support with two outcome-based achievements (not hours worked), acknowledge the market context with one data point, and then stop talking.
success_criteria: Specific number anchored in the first sentence
Ends cleanly — no backtracking or hedging after the ask

title: The Job Interview — First 60 Seconds
carnegie_principle: Interviewers form their hiring instinct in the first 90 seconds. The rest of the interview is confirmation. The first 60 seconds must establish warmth, competence, and genuine interest — in that order.
modern_context: Research on hiring decisions consistently shows that interviewers make an initial judgement rapidly and then seek evidence to confirm it. The first minute is disproportionately weighted.
practice_prompt: Record your opening 60 seconds for a job interview for a senior role in your field. Warm greeting, one specific reason you're excited about this company (not generic), one outcome from your career that signals competence, then turn it back to them with a question.
success_criteria: Company-specific reason for interest — not 'great culture'
Ends with a genuine question to the interviewer

title: Negotiating a Job Offer
carnegie_principle: The offer is the opening position, not the final one. Every component is negotiable — salary, equity, start date, remote flexibility, title, review timeline. Most people negotiate only salary and leave everything else on the table.
modern_context: Studies consistently show that 85% of hiring managers have room to negotiate beyond their first offer, and almost no candidates ask. The cost of asking is near zero; the gain compounds over decades.
practice_prompt: You've received a job offer 12% below your target. Record a 90-second negotiation response that: thanks them genuinely, anchors your counter on salary with a market reference, and adds one non-salary item you want without making either feel like a demand.
success_criteria: Gratitude expressed without weakening the position
Counter-offer anchored with a market reference

title: Managing Up — Disagreeing with Your Boss
carnegie_principle: Disagreeing with your manager is a leadership skill, not insubordination. The manager who only hears agreement is being managed into bad decisions by people optimising for comfort.
modern_context: The most valuable team members push back with evidence and alternatives, not complaints. Managing up effectively requires timing, framing, and a concrete alternative — not just a 'no'.
practice_prompt: Your manager has made a project decision you believe is wrong. You have 48 hours before it's announced. Record a 60-second private conversation opener: express disagreement clearly, give one specific reason with evidence, and propose one alternative.
success_criteria: Disagreement expressed directly, not hinted at
Respects the manager's authority while challenging the decision

title: Handling a Difficult Boss
carnegie_principle: A difficult boss is a communication problem before it is a personnel problem. Most 'difficult boss' situations are resolved by better diagnosis, clearer expectations, and more deliberate relationship management.
modern_context: Research on upward management shows that most manager-report conflicts stem from unclear expectations, different work styles, or unspoken assumptions — all of which are addressable through conversation.
practice_prompt: Your manager is a micromanager who doesn't trust your judgement. Record a 90-second conversation where you raise the dynamic directly and propose a concrete system for building trust — without being defensive or accusatory.
success_criteria: Names the dynamic specifically, not vaguely
Invites their perspective on what would help

title: Presenting to a Hostile Room
carnegie_principle: A hostile audience is not a failed presentation — it is a presentation with higher stakes and a different opening requirement. The first move is always to demonstrate that you understand why they're hostile.
modern_context: Town halls during layoffs, strategy pivots, or culture changes routinely produce hostile rooms. The presenters who succeed are those who name the elephant, not those who ignore it.
practice_prompt: You're presenting a major reorg to a team that didn't see it coming and is understandably upset. Record a 90-second opening that names the emotional reality in the room before presenting any content.
success_criteria: Emotional reality of the room named explicitly in the first 20 seconds
No content introduced before the acknowledgement is complete
Tone is steady and warm, not defensive or apologetic

title: Cold Outreach That Gets a Reply
carnegie_principle: A cold message gets a reply when it is personal, brief, makes one specific ask, and makes it easy to say yes. Everything else is noise.
modern_context: The average professional receives dozens of cold outreach messages a week. The ones that get replies are specific about why this person, why now, and what specifically is being asked.
practice_prompt: You want 20 minutes with a senior leader you've never met. Record a 60-second voicemail — the written equivalent — that is so specific, so brief, and so clearly low-effort to respond to that ignoring it would feel rude.
success_criteria: Specific reason for choosing this person

title: Delivering Bad News
carnegie_principle: Bad news delivered directly, early, and with a clear path forward preserves trust. Bad news delivered slowly, cushioned, or optimistically packaged destroys it.
modern_context: The 'bad news sandwich' (positive-negative-positive) is widely taught and widely distrusted. People can sense when the positive bookends are performative. Direct delivery with genuine empathy is more credible.
practice_prompt: A project you're responsible for has failed. Record a 60-second update to your stakeholder group: the news is delivered in the first sentence, the cause in two sentences, and the path forward in two more. No sandwich.
success_criteria: Bad news in the first sentence
Path forward is concrete and owned
No positive opener or closer that contradicts the message

title: Networking Without Feeling Fake
carnegie_principle: Authentic networking is giving before asking, being genuinely curious, and thinking in decades not transactions. The people who are 'good at networking' are the people who are genuinely interested in other people.
modern_context: Most people feel fake at networking events because they're thinking about what they want to get. Shifting to genuine curiosity — not as a strategy but as a practice — produces both better connections and less discomfort.
practice_prompt: Record a 90-second networking conversation opener with someone at a professional event. You know only that they work in a different part of your industry. Lead with genuine curiosity, find one real point of connection, and resist mentioning what you do for as long as possible.
success_criteria: Three genuine questions about the other person before talking about yourself
One authentic point of connection identified
Your own role mentioned last, not first

title: The Client Escalation Call
carnegie_principle: A client who escalates is a client who still believes resolution is possible. The first job is to restore that belief — before any problem-solving begins.
modern_context: Escalation calls fail when the company representative starts defending immediately. They succeed when the representative leads with full acknowledgement of the impact, takes ownership, and only then moves to solutions.
practice_prompt: A major client calls to say they're considering leaving because of a series of delivery failures. Record a 90-second opening that leads with acknowledgement, takes specific ownership, and earns the right to propose a solution.
success_criteria: Acknowledgement comes before any explanation or defence
Specific ownership taken — not 'the team' or 'circumstances'
Solution not introduced until acknowledgement is complete
Tone is calm and direct, not panicked

title: Returning to Work After a Break
carnegie_principle: Re-entering a professional environment after a career break requires proactive narrative control — your story, told your way, before others fill in the gaps.
modern_context: Career gaps are increasingly common and increasingly accepted — but only when the person re-entering owns the narrative confidently. Apologetic framing signals insecurity; direct, purposeful framing signals self-awareness.
practice_prompt: Record a 60-second response to 'So, tell me about the gap in your CV.' The break was 18 months. Frame it directly, own what you did with it, and pivot to what you bring now — without apology.
success_criteria: Gap named directly in the first sentence
Pivot to current value in the final 20 seconds
